questions.question: Define IoT and list three real-world examples of IoT devices with their applications.
keyPoints: IoT is the network of physical objects embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity to exchange data over the internet.
Smart Thermostat (e.g., Nest) — monitors room temperature and controls HVAC systems automatically.
Wearable Fitness Tracker (e.g., Fitbit) — monitors heart rate, steps, and sleep patterns.
Industrial Predictive Maintenance Sensor — monitors machine vibration to predict failures before they occur.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the Physical Design and Logical Design of an IoT system.
keyPoints: Physical Design refers to the tangible hardware: sensors (detect environment), actuators (perform actions), microcontrollers (process data), and communication modules (connect to network).
It is concerned with device specifications, power management, and physical connectivity.
Logical Design defines the six functional blocks: Device, Communication, Services, Management, Application, and Security.
It describes the flow of data, communication protocols (MQTT, HTTP, CoAP), and the system's software architecture, independent of specific hardware.

questions.question: Explain the four IoT communication models with a suitable example and use case for each.
keyPoints: Request-Response (e.g., HTTP): Client sends a request, server processes and returns a response. Used in web-based IoT dashboards querying device status.

Publish-Subscribe (e.g., MQTT): Publishers send messages to topics on a broker; subscribers receive messages from topics they subscribe to. Most important for IoT — used in sensor networks where many devices report data intermittently.
Push-Pull (e.g., ZeroMQ): Producers push messages into a queue, consumers pull from it. Decouples producers from consumers. Used in data pipeline architectures.

Exclusive Pair (e.g., WebSockets): A bidirectional, stateful, full-duplex communication channel between client and server. Used for real-time control applications requiring continuous two-way communication.

questions.question: Describe any four of the six IoT levels with a real-world example for each.
keyPoints: Level 1: Single device performs all functions (sensing, analysis, control). Example: Smart night lamp with a light sensor that turns on/off automatically.
Level 3: Single node senses and sends data to cloud, which can send commands back to an actuator. Example: Smart thermostat controlled via a mobile app.
Level 5: Multiple sensing nodes, multiple actuators, and a coordinator node that aggregates data before sending to the cloud. Example: Industrial automation system on a factory floor.
Level 6: Distributed mesh network of independent nodes that can function locally without the cloud. Example: Smart city sensor network.
